coredata.dc:title: Cathodic electrochemiluminescence of boron and nitrogen-codoped carbon dots for the detection of dissolved oxygen in seawater
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Hongye
Zhang, Yifei
Gao, Wenyue
Wu, Chi
coredata.dc:description: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is widely used in various fields due to its high sensitivity and controllable characteristics. Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as promising ECL emitters due to their simple synthesis, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility. However, the practical application of many CDs emitters is hindered by their limited luminous efficiency, often necessitating additional coreactants to enhance the ECL signal intensity. In this study, we synthesized boron and nitrogen-codoped carbon dots (BN-CDs) as ECL emitters, utilizing dissolved oxygen (DO) as the coreactant. The BN-CDs/DO system exhibited a strong cathodic ECL signal. We proposed a reaction mechanism for the BN-CDs/DO ECL system. Additionally, we developed an ECL sensor for DO detection based on this system, showing a linear correlation between ECL peak intensity and DO concentration from 0.5 to 19.8 mg/L, with a detection limit of 0.12 mg/L. It was proven reliable for DO analysis in seawater and freshwater environments. This study provides insights into the synthesis and utilization of BN-CDs, highlighting the potential of DO as an intrinsic coreactant in CDs ECL systems. Furthermore, it provides new perspectives on the detection of DO in seawater and the design of innovative DO sensors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM of BN-CDs, (B) FT-IR spectra of N-CDs and BN-CDs, (C) XRD patterns of BN-CDs, and (D) Raman spectra of BN-CDs.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectra and Fluorescence spectra of BN-CDs. (B) FL spectra of BN-CDs and ECL spectra of BN-CDs in 0.1 M PBS under air saturation.
(A) ECL responses of BN-CDs in N2, air and O2-saturated solution (PMT voltage, 800 V). (B) ECL intensity variation with the addition of 1.0 mM IPA and BQ as radical scavengers (PMT voltage, 800 V).

(A) The varied ECL signals in 0.1 M PBS with DO concentration (mg/L) ranging from a to s (a: 0.1, b: 0.5, c: 1.5, d: 2.3, e: 3.0, f: 4.3, g: 5.2, h: 6.8, i: 7.5, j: 8.0, k: 9.0, l: 9.8, m: 11.3, n: 12.1, o: 13.5, p: 15.0, q: 15.8, r: 17.5, s: 19.8.) (B) The linear relationship between the DO concentration and ECL peak intensity (potential range: from −4 V to 0 V; scan rate: 0.5 V/s; PMT voltage, 800 V).
(A) Preparation of BN-CDs, and (B) for the ECL sensing of DO.
The comparison results of the presented ECL sensor with a commercial optical sensor for DO determination in water samples (n = 3).

row.entry:
Determined by a commercial optical sensor (HACH)
(mg/L)

head.ce:title: Cathodic electrochemiluminescence of boron and nitrogen-codoped carbon dots for the detection of dissolved oxygen in seawater

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Provincial Center for In-Situ Marine Sensors
Institute of Marine Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is widely used in various fields due to its high sensitivity and controllable characteristics. Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as promising ECL emitters due to their simple synthesis, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility. However, the practical application of many CDs emitters is hindered by their limited luminous efficiency, often necessitating additional coreactants to enhance the ECL signal intensity. In this study, we synthesized boron and nitrogen-codoped carbon dots (BN-CDs) as ECL emitters, utilizing dissolved oxygen (DO) as the coreactant. The BN-CDs/DO system exhibited a strong cathodic ECL signal. We proposed a reaction mechanism for the BN-CDs/DO ECL system. Additionally, we developed an ECL sensor for DO detection based on this system, showing a linear correlation between ECL peak intensity and DO concentration from 0.5 to 19.8 mg/L, with a detection limit of 0.12 mg/L. It was proven reliable for DO analysis in seawater and freshwater environments. This study provides insights into the synthesis and utilization of BN-CDs, highlighting the potential of DO as an intrinsic coreactant in CDs ECL systems. Furthermore, it provides new perspectives on the detection of DO in seawater and the design of innovative DO sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dissolved oxygen (DO) refers to oxygen molecules dissolved in water, serving as a vital indicator of water quality and a key element in water purification [1]. Monitoring the DO content in seawater allows for assessing the marine ecosystem's health and timely detecting hypoxia or oxygen deficiency, safeguarding the marine environment [2]. This is crucial for marine environmental protection, management, and climate change research [3]. Over recent decades, various methods and sensors have been developed for measuring DO concentration, including colorimetry [4], electrochemistry [5], and fluorescence quenching [6]. However, these approaches are limited by time-consuming procedures, complex operations, and high costs. Hence, there is an urgent demand for a novel analytical method capable of achieving ultra-sensitive detection of DO.

Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) offers advantages such as high sensitivity, ease of operation, and controllability. It is widely used in fields such as chemical and biochemical sensing [7], food monitoring [8], environmental detection [9], and clinical diagnosis [10]. Common coreactants in ECL, such as S2O8 2− or H2O2, suffer from biotoxicity and instability [11], making them less desirable for analytical applications. Dissolved oxygen (DO), a safe and effective coreactant agent, has garnered significant interest from researchers. However, the low decomposition rate of DO often requires employing a ternary ECL system (luminescent/DO/coreaction accelerator) to achieve a significant ECL intensity for detection [12]. The addition of these coreaction accelerators may lead to interfering reactions with other electroactive substances in biological system [13]. Consequently, many researchers are actively exploring efficient binary ECL systems (luminophores/DO) without coreaction accelerators [14]. Meanwhile, ECL holds potential for detecting DO due to the crucial role of oxygen in numerous ECL reactions [15,16]. Lin et al. [17] previously advocated for the use of ECL sensors in DO detection. Nonetheless, existing DO ECL sensors rely on heavy metal-based quantum dots as ECL emitters, leading to high costs and suboptimal biocompatibility.

Carbon dots (CDs) are novel ECL emitters known for their simple synthesis, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility, attracting significant attention from researchers [18]. However, the ECL efficiency of CDs remains relatively low. Heteroatom doping has been recognized as an efficient strategy for modifying the photoelectric characteristics, surface states, and local chemical properties of CDs [19,20]. These heteroatoms can be derived from precursors during the synthesis process. Among many possibilities, nitrogen (N) stands out as an efficient dopant. N-doping introduces electrons into the conduction band, elevates the Fermi energy level of CDs, and creates additional surface traps, thus accelerating electron transfer between the coreactant and CDs [21]. Consequently, N-doped CDs (N-CDs) typically exhibit enhanced ECL efficiency compared to non-doped CDs. Additionally, codoping multiple heteroatoms has attracted considerable attention. This approach generates a unique electronic structure in CDs due to the synergistic effects between the doped heteroatoms [22]. The electronegativity difference between boron (B) and oxygen (O) allows direct attachment of oxygen molecules to boron atoms, facilitating subsequent oxygen reduction. Furthermore, researchers have revealed that the introduction of B atoms into BN-CDs results in their conversion into boron radicals (B•) through electrically exciting and accelerating coreactants to generate abundant active radicals [23]. These processes enable more efficient energy and electron transfer reactions, resulting in enhanced ECL signals.

Herein, we developed a novel ECL system with BN-CDs as luminophores and DO as a coreactant, as illustrated in Scheme 1 . Surface functionalization of CDs was achieved through a one-pot hydrothermal synthesis process employing the doping method, resulting in highly efficient BN-CDs as ECL emitters. The BN-CDs generate cathodic ECL signals under negative potential in the presence of DO. Using DO as a coreactant avoids the need for other reagents, crucial in certain ECL analyses and ECL bioimaging applications. The reaction mechanism of the BN-CDs/DO ECL system was discussed in detail, offering inspiration for both the synthesis and utilization of surface-functionalized CDs. Based on this, we proposed the first ECL system for DO detection using CDs as luminophores. Experimental results demonstrated a strong linear correlation between the ECL peak intensity of this DO sensor and DO concentration from 0.5 to 19.8 mg/L, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.12 mg/L (S/N = 3). This system was successfully employed for the analysis of DO in complex offshore seawater and other aqueous environments, validating the reliability of the sensor and presenting a novel approach for DO detection.

BN-CDs were synthesized via hydrothermal treatment (Scheme 1A) with slight modifications to the reported method [24]. First, 0.96 g of citric acid (CA) and 0.31 g of boric acid (BA) were dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water. Next, 335 μL of ethylenediamine (EDA) was added dropwise with vigorous stirring. After 5 min, the mixture was transferred to a 50 mL autoclave and heated at 210 °C for 8 h in a furnace, resulting in a dark brown liquid. The resulting solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (1000 Da) at 4 °C for 72 h, shielded from light, and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter for subsequent use. The synthesis of CDs, boron-doped carbon dots (B-CDs), and nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) utilized CA, CA and BA, and CA and EDA as precursors, respectively. The procedures described above were applied consistently.

The synthesized BN-CDs were introduced into a phosphate buffer solution (PBS) at a 1:32 ratio (BN-CDs) for ECL measurements with DO as the coreactant. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was used to induce ECL by conducting potential scans from 0 V to −4 V at a scan rate of 0.5 V/s. The high voltage of the photomultiplier tube (PMT) was set to 900 V, unless otherwise specified.

O2 and N2 were introduced into a gas regulating proportioner to generate a gas mixture with adjustable proportions. The gas mixture was directed to a gas tank, and the tank output was connected to a gas flow meter to achieve adjustable air pressure. Varying the O2 to N2 ratio allows simulation of an aqueous solution with different DO concentrations. After saturating the solution with gas (typically by introducing the gas mixture for 10 min into 10 mL of solution), the DO concentration was determined. The DO concentration was monitored using a commercial DO meter (HQ2100, HACH) as a reference.

Samples were collected from laboratory tap water, drinking mineral water, artificial lake water at Shandong University (Qingdao), and offshore seawater in Qingdao, China. The seawater sample was collected in July, while the other three samples were taken in December. Before use, the lake and seawater samples were filtered using a microporous nitrocellulose membrane with a pore size of 0.45 μm.

The morphology of BN-CDs was characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 1 A illustrates the monodispersed particles with an average diameter of 5.3 ± 0.8 nm by measuring more than 100 particles, indicating the high dispersity and homogeneity of the prepared BN-CDs. The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) measurements show that the BN-CDs have a crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) facet of graphite [25].

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was used to record the IR absorption spectra of N-CDs and BN-CDs. Fig. 1B shows characteristic peaks at 3258, 2938, 1644, 1545, and 1386 cm−1, corresponding to O–H, C–H, CO, CC, and C–N stretching, respectively, observed in both N-CDs and BN-CDs. New peaks appeared in BN-CDs at 1356 cm−1 (B–N stretching), 1154 cm−1 (B–C stretching), and 1048 cm−1 (B–O stretching), confirming successful B doping [26]. Moreover, the sharp bands at 777 and 1432 cm−1, characteristic of h-BN, were observed [27]. Additionally, the elemental composition of BN-CDs was further confirmed through energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) characterization and element mapping. As shown in Fig. S1, the elements C, O, N, and B are evenly distributed. The X-ray diffraction pattern of BN-CDs (Fig. 1C) exhibited a broad diffraction peak centered at approximately 2θ = 25°, corresponding to the (002) plane [3,28,29]. Furthermore, the Raman spectra of N-CDs and BN-CDs (Fig. 1D) exhibit two signals at 1353/1340 cm−1 (D-band) and 1564/1567 cm−1 (G-band) under 532 nm excitation. The G-band is attributed to the in-plane stretching vibration of the C atoms’ sp2 hybridization, while the D-band corresponds to vibrations of disordered or defective carbons, including intrinsic carbon defects and heteroatom substitution defects. Raman spectra of N-CDs and BN-CDs under 532 nm excitation revealed an increased intensity ratio of the D-band (lattice defects) to the G-band (highly ordered graphite) (ID/IG), from 1.07 (N-CDs) to 1.56 (BN-CDs). It suggests that B doping on the CDs induces more defects.

Furthermore, the elemental composition and surface chemical properties of BN-CDs were identified using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Peaks at 191.8, 284.8, 399.8, and 531.0 eV in the spectra (Fig. S2A) correspond to B 1s, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. Additionally, the high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. S2B) exhibits five characteristic fitted peaks at 283.8 eV (B–C), 284.5 eV (CC), 285.7 eV (C–N), 286.5 eV (C–O), and 287.6 eV (CO). In Figs. S2C and S2D, the N and B elements are primarily doped in the form of pyridinic-N (399.5 eV) and partially oxidized B (192.1 eV).

The optical properties of BN-CDs were examined via UV–visible absorption, fluorescence (FL), and ECL spectroscopy. Fig. 2 A shows absorption peaks at 218 and 337 nm for BN-CDs, indicating the presence of sp2-carbon and the n-π* transition of CO [30]. Additionally, the maximum FL emission (425 nm) was observed at an optimal excitation wavelength of 339 nm. The maximum ECL emission wavelength (630 nm) was red-shifted by 205 nm compared to the FL emission of BN-CDs (Fig. 2B). This red-shift could be attributed to the difference between the ECL surface-state emission and the FL intrinsic-state emission [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL response and mechanism of the BN-CDs/DO system
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical activities and ECL responses of CDs, B-CDs, N-CDs, and BN-CDs were investigated and compared at the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) in aqueous solutions containing DO. As shown in Fig. S3, all CDs demonstrated similar electrochemical behaviors, with strong reduction currents occurring at an onset potential of −2.0 V. The performance of the electrochemical reduction was improved through additional B doping. The peak reduction currents of the prepared BN-CDs and B-CDs were slightly higher than those of CDs and N-CDs, with BN-CDs showing the highest electroactivity, confirming the synergistic effect of B and N in binary doping. Additionally, the cathodic ECL signals of CDs and B-CDs are extremely weak and negligible compared to those of N-CDs and BN-CDs. The ECL peak signal of BN-CDs at −3.05 V is 2.6 times stronger than that of N-CDs. Doping CDs with EDA, a commonly used nitrogen source and surface-passivating agent, enhances the ECL emission of CDs by preventing the nonradiative recombination of excitons [32]. In the past decade, studies have reported that carbon nanomaterials co-doped with B and N exhibit high electrocatalytic activity for redox reactions due to a synergistic effect. This is because B at the edges of BN-CDs can be electrically excited to produce B•, which catalyze O2 to generate more reactive oxygen species (ROS) [33,34]. Furthermore, in N-doped carbon materials, the higher electronegativity of N compared to C induces positive charges on carbon atoms. Additional doping with B counteracts this effect, increasing the asymmetric spin density of carbon atoms and thereby enhancing the ORR activity of the material. It is worth noting that when a higher potential was applied, hydrogen and oxygen bubbles were generated on the working electrode and platinum counter electrode surfaces due to water electrolysis. Due to the high potential scanning rate and short electric excitation time, the effects of oxygen and hydrogen on the system were considered negligible. Additionally, subsequent experimental results showed satisfactory accuracy and sensitivity.

To investigate the influence of DO on the ECL reaction, we studied the ECL behaviors of BN-CDs under different DO concentrations. As shown in Fig. 3 A, ECL peak signals appeared in the air-saturated PBS solution with BN-CDs. However, when DO was removed by bubbling N2 into the solution, the ECL peak intensity decreased dramatically. Upon saturating with O2, the ECL signals recovered and were much higher than in the air-saturated solution. This suggests that DO, as an important coreactant, efficiently enhances ECL signals, and the ECL intensity depends on the concentration of DO.

DO-related ECL reaction mechanisms generally involve ROS, including OH•, O2 •-, and other free radical derivatives, which act as crucial intermediates leading to luminescence. To confirm the active intermediates in the ECL reactions, we studied the impact of various active oxygen radical scavengers on the ECL signal of BN-CDs/O2 systems. Isopropyl alcohol (IPA) is commonly used as an OH• radical scavenger, while benzoquinone (BQ) acts as an O2 •- radical anion scavenger. As displayed in Fig. 3B, the emission intensities of ECL decreased by 91 % and 38 % of the original value when 1.0 mM BQ and IPA were added to BN-CDs solutions, respectively. The significant reduction in the ECL signal with the addition of BQ confirms the critical role of O2 •- in the ECL reaction.

Based on our experimental results and reported investigations, we outline the ECL enhancement mechanism of the BN-CDs/DO system as follows: Initially, O2 and BN-CDs are reduced to O2 •- and BN-CDs radical anions (BN-CDs•-), respectively (eqs (1) and (2)). The difference in electronegativity between B and O atoms facilitates the adsorption of O2 molecules onto B atoms, resulting in subsequent reduction of O2. Additionally, the electronegativity difference between B, N, and C atoms leads to the catalytic reduction of O2 by B• in CDs, generating more O2 •- [35] (eq (3)). The reaction between BN-CDs•- and O2 •- leads to the formation of excited BN-CDs* (eq (4)). Lastly, a robust ECL emission occurs through the transition of excited BN-CDs* to ground state BN-CDs (eq (5)).

Electrochemical reduction:
(1) O2 + e− → O2 •-
(2) BN-CDs + e− →BN-CDs•-

B• catalytic reaction:
(3) B• + OH− + O2 → O2 •- + BOH

ECL emission:
(4) 2BN-CDs•- + 3O2 •- + 6H2O → 2BN-CDs*+12OH-
(5) BN-CDs* → BN-CDs + hv

The ECL characteristics of CDs depend on the structures of the synthesized materials. CA, either alone or most commonly in combination with N-containing compounds, can produce various CDs with diverse structures and photoelectric properties. In this synthesis, CA was chosen as the precursor, EDA as the nitrogen dopant source and passivating agent, and BA as the boron dopant source. The structure and surface functionalization of BN-CDs were controlled by adjusting the molar ratio of starting materials, hydrothermal reaction temperature, reaction time, and dialysis duration. Detailed information on the ECL responses of BN-CDs prepared under various conditions is provided in the supporting materials and Fig. S4.

We further investigated the influence of experimental parameters, such as solution pH and BN-CD concentration, on the ECL responses of the BN-CDs/DO system. As shown in Fig. S5A, the ECL emission of BN-CDs is influenced by solution pH. The ECL signal increases from pH 6 to 8.2, then decreases at pH higher than 8.2 up to 11. At lower pH levels, protons in the solution are easily reduced at negative potentials, hindering the competitive reduction of oxygen and subsequently affecting the ECL intensity. Conversely, in solutions with high pH, CDs tend to aggregate through non-covalent interactions [36], resulting in decreased luminescence efficiency. For practical applications, a pH of 8.2 was chosen to achieve an optimal balance between ECL intensity and prevention of CD aggregation.

The quantity of BN-CDs used has a significant influence on the ECL intensity (Fig. S5B). Diluting BN-CDs in air-saturated PBS initially increased ECL steadily up to a 1:32 BN-CDs: PBS ratio; however, it decreased at higher concentrations. The decrease in ECL intensity at higher concentrations may be attributed to nanoparticle agglomeration, forming larger nanoclusters due to van der Waals forces [37]. The size effect of these clusters reduces the photovoltaic properties of CDs, and high CDs loading on the electrode may hinder electron transfer and interactions with ROS [38]. Therefore, a 1:32 BN-CDs: PBS ratio was chosen for practical applications.

After optimizing the experimental conditions, we quantitatively detected DO using the prepared ECL sensor. As depicted in Fig. 4 A, the ECL response exhibited a linear increase with DO concentration, ranging from 0.5 to 19.8 mg/L. The regression equation was I
ECL = 90.37 + 434.57 c
DO (R2 = 0.9986). The LOD for DO was calculated to be 0.12 mg/L (S/N = 3) and a limit of quantification (LOQ) of 0.40 mg/L (S/N = 10). Compared to other reported DO sensors (Table S1), the proposed DO sensor based on the ECL technique stands out for its simplicity, sensitivity, and innovative approach to DO determination.

The anti-interference performance of this DO sensor was systematically evaluated in the presence of several common ions and molecules, including K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Sr2+, Cl−, SO4 2−, Br−, CO3 2−, F−, H3BO3, urea, and glucose. These possible interferents are commonly found as primary constituents in seawater. As shown in Fig. S6, even at a high concentration of 0.1 mM, these substances exhibited minimal interference in DO detection. The BN-CDs/DO ECL system showed robust resistance to interference from these common substances, even at high concentrations. These results provide compelling evidence of the sensitivity and selectivity of the BN-CDs/DO ECL system for detecting DO.

To further evaluate the applicability of the proposed ECL sensor, DO measurements were conducted in four types of water samples: tap water, mineral water, lake water, and seawater. Initially, a calibration curve (Figs. S7–S10) was generated by introducing different concentrations of oxygen into a water test solution. Subsequently, a distinct portion of each water sample was analyzed to quantify the DO concentration. The measurement results are presented in Table 1 . The accuracy was calculated as:
(6)
Accuracy = C/C
R × 100%

where C and C
R represent the DO concentration (mg/L) determined by the ECL sensor and a commercial optical sensor, respectively. The close agreement between the DO values obtained with our proposed sensor and those from a commercially available optical DO sensor demonstrates the high accuracy of our sensor for online DO detection. Notably, the seawater samples collected in summer were not autoclaved, which allowed microorganisms to persistently consume DO, resulting in lower observed values.

In summary, an ECL system employing BN-CDs as the emitter and DO as coreactant was innovatively designed and experimentally demonstrated. The BN-CDs exhibit significantly enhanced ECL efficiency compared to both non-doped and singly-doped CDs, owing to the synergistic effect of boron (B) and nitrogen (N). The ECL intensity of this system demonstrates a positive correlation with the DO concentration. This sensing strategy was successfully applied to detect DO concentration in seawater. Although further investigation and improvement are needed to address the relatively high excitation potential required by the CDs/DO ECL system, this study showcases the promising potential of using novel CDs in ECL applications, eliminating the need for additional coreactants or coreaction accelerators.

Hongye Yang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yifei Zhang: Investigation, Data curation. Wenyue Gao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Chi Wu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant number 22104072); the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (grant number ZR2021QB019); the Outstanding Youth Science Foundation of Shandong Province (Overseas) (grant number 2022HWYQ-058); Southern Marine Science and Engineering Guangdong Laboratory (Guangzhou) (GML2021GD0808); and the Shandong University Foundation for Future Scholar Plan.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrogen bond organic frameworks as radical reactors for enhancement in ECL efficiency and their ultrasensitive biosensing
